THE
INDIA LIST
AND
INDIA OFFICE LIST

FOR

1897.

COMPILED FROM OFFICIAL RECORDS BY DIRECTION OF THE SECRETARY
OF STATE FOR INDIA IN COUNCIL.

LONDON:
HARRISON AND SONS, 59, PALL MALL,
BOOKSELLERS TO HER MAJESTY AND H.R.H. THE PRINCE OF WALES.

1897.

Price Ten Shillings and Sixpence.

PREFACE.

In the lists of Officers serving in India, which are contained in pp. 7 to 101 of the present volume, the names of Officers drawing substantive pay of less than Rs. 500 a month are not included.

In the Record of Services (pp. 353 to 536) a different principle of selection has been followed, as explained on p. 351.

Intimations of errors in the lists, or of omissions, should be addressed to the Editor of the India List, India Office, S.W.

THE INDIA OFFICE LIST.

OFFICE OF

THE SECRETARY OF STATE FOR INDIA

IN COUNCIL,

ST. JAMES'S PARK, S.W.

SECRETARY OF STATE.

THE RIGHT HON. LORD GEORGE HAMILTON, M.P.

UNDER SECRETARIES OF STATE.

Sir Arthur Godley, K.C.B. ; The Earl of Onslow, G.C.M.G.

ASSISTANT UNDER SECRETARY OF STATE.

Sir Horace G. Walpole, K.C.B., J.P.

COUNCIL.

Vice-President.—Sir Charles Arthur Turner, K.C.I.E.

Field-Marshal Sir Donald M. Stewart, Bart.,
G.C.B., G.C.S.I., C.I.E.		Sir James Braithwaite Peile, K.C.S.I.
Gen. Sir Archibald Alison, Bart., G.C.B.
R. Hardie.Sir Charles H. T. Crosthwaite, K.C.S.I.
Sir Alexander J. Arbuthnot, K.C.S.I., C.I.E.Sir Stuart C. Bayley, K.C.S.I., C.I.E.
Sir Alfred Comyns Lyall, K.C.B., G.C.I.E.F. C. Le Marchant.
Gen. J. J. H. Gordon, C.B.

Clerk of the Council.—Sir Horace G. Walpole, K.C.B., J.P.

Private Secretary to the Secretary of State.—R. T. W. Ritchie.

Assistant Private Secretary and Précis Writer.—F. T. C. Hastings.

Political A.D.C. to the Secretary of State.—Sir W. G. S. V. Fitzgerald, K.C.I.E., C.S.I.

Private Secretary to Sir Arthur Godley.—Francis C. Drake.

Private Secretary to Lord Onslow.—John E. Ferard.

Reading Clerk to the Council.—Colin G. Campbell.

Resident Clerks.—W. Robinson and F. A. Hirtzel.

CORRESPONDENCE DEPARTMENTS.Secretaries.

Financial.—Sir Henry Waterfield, K.C.S.I., C.B.
Judicial and Public.—Sir Philip P. Hutchins, K.C.S.I.
Military.—Maj.-Gen. Sir Oliver R. Newmarch, K.C.S.I.
Political and Secret.—W. Lee-Warner, C.S.I.
Public Works.—Clement S. Colvin.
Revenue and Statistics.—Sir Charles Edward Bernard, K.C.S.I.

Assistants.

Financial.—Stapleton C. Hogg.
Judicial and Public.—W. Neville Sturt.
Military.—F. Whitmore Smith.
Political and Secret.—Edmund Neel, C.I.E.
Public Works.—Henry Hill.
Revenue and Statistics.—Arthur N. Wollaston, C.I.E.

Clerks.

Senior Clerks.—Charles N. B. Franks, Francis B. Armstrong, Frederick C. Thompson, James H. Seabrooke, Colin G. Campbell, Patrick H. C. Herbert, Francis W. Newmarch, Richmond T. W. Ritchie, Hermann A. Haines, Edward Franks, Henry L. Seccombe, Henry Ventris, and Ashley F. Brereton.

Clerk specially attached to Political and Secret Department.—F. T. C. Hastings.
Clerk for Examination of Army Non-Effective Accounts.—Walter J. Greene.
Technical Assistant and Clerk to Special Assistant and Visitor to the India Museum.—B. J. Rose.
Staff Clerk.—S. Keith.
Second Class Clerks (Higher Grade).—H. W. Garrett, W. Stantiall, and W. H. Swain.
Second Class Clerks.—E. S. Hopkins, G. Ashton, E. W. Sutton, J. Putnam, A. Phibbs, W. J. Bowden, H. F. Mackay, and J. Roxburgh.
Clerk specially attached to Financial Department.—H. C. Bellingham.

ROYAL INDIAN ENGINEERING COLLEGE, COOPER'S HILL.
BOARD OF VISITORS.CHAIRMAN.

Sir Richard Temple, Bart., G.C.S.I., C.I.E., D.C.L.

W. H. Barlow, F.R.S., Past President of the Institution of Civil Engineers.
John Wolfe Barry, C.B., F.R.S., President of the Institution of Civil Engineers.
Sir Dietrich Brandis, Ph.D., K.C.I.E., F.R.S., late Inspector-General of Forests to the Government of India.
Sir Juland Danvers, K.C.S.I.

Sir J. Fowler, K.C.M.G., Past President of the Institution of Civil Engineers.
Hugh Leonard, Esq., late Chief Engineer, P.W.D., India, Fellow, and President of the Faculty of Engineering, Calcutta University.
W. H. Preece, C.B., F.R.S., M.Inst.C.E.
Sir Alexander M. Rendel, K.C.I.E., M.A., M.Inst.C.E.

STAFF OF THE COLLEGE.

PRESIDENT.—Col. J. Pennycuick, C.S.I., R.E.

Secretary.—Lieut.-Colonel W. J. Boyes.

Bursar.—J. P. Pasco, R.N., Retired.

Chaplain.—Rev. C. Crosleigh, D.D.

Medical Officer.—H. E. Giffard.

ENGINEERING AND APPLIED MECHANICS BRANCHES.

Professor of Engineering Construction.—Callcott Reilly, M.Inst.C.E.

Professor of Hydraulic Engineering and Mechanism.—T. A. Hearson, M.Inst.C.E.

Assistant Professor of Engineering.—A. H. Heath, Assoc. Mem. Inst. C.E.

Professor of Surveying.—Major-Gen. E. H. Courtney, R.E.

Instructor in Surveying.—R. J. Woods, M.E., F.C.H., Assoc. Mem. Inst. C.E.

Instructor in Geometrical Drawing and in Estimating, and Lecturer in Architecture.—A. Hicks.

Assistant Instructor in Geometrical Drawing.—C. B. McElwee.

Lecturer in Accounts.—J. C. Hurst.

Demonstrator in the Mechanical Laboratory.—Philip Reilly.

MATHEMATICAL BRANCH.

Professor of Applied Mathematics.—G. M. Minchin, M.A., F.R.S.

Professor of Pure Mathematics.—A. Lodge, M.A., Fellow of St. John's College, Oxford.

NATURAL SCIENCE BRANCH.

Professor of Chemistry.—Herbert McLeod, F.R.S.

Demonstrator in Chemistry.—F. E. Matthews, Ph.D.

Analytical Chemist.—F. W. Harbord, Assoc. Royal School of Mines, F.I.C.

Professor of Physics.—W. N. Stocker, M.A., Fellow of Brasenose College, Oxford.

Demonstrator in Physics.—T. Shields, M.A., B.Sc.

Lecturer in Geology and Mineralogy.—Professor H. G. Seeley, F.R.S.

FORESTRY BRANCH.

Principal Professor of Forestry.—W. Schlich, C.I.E., Ph.D., F.L.S., late Inspector-General of Forests to the Government of India.

Assistant Professor of Forestry.—W. R. Fisher, B.A., Conservator of Forests, Indian Forest Service.

Lecturer on Botany.—C. A. Barber, M.A.

Lecturer on Entomology.—W. H. Blandford, M.A.

Lecturer on Organic Chemistry.—A. H. Church, M.A., F.R.S.

Instructor in French.—J. A. Perret.

Instructor in German.—T. H. Dittel.

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA.

VICEROY AND GOVERNOR-GENERAL OF INDIA.

His Excellency the Right Hon. Victor Alexander Bruce, EARL OF ELGIN AND KINCARDINE, P.C., G.M.S.I., G.M.I.E. 27 January, 1894.

PERSONAL STAFF OF THE GOVERNOR-GENERAL

Private Secretary, H. Babington Smith.

Assistant Private Secretary, F. W. Latimer.

Military Secretary, Colonel A. G. A. Durand, C.B.

Aides-de-Camp, Captain R. E. Grimston; Lieutenant S. H. Pollen; Lieut. F. L. Adam; and Lieut. Viscount Fincastle.

Extra Aides-de-Camp, Captain R. W. Morley, Lieut. Lord G. Stewart-Murray, Capt. M. H. K. Pechell, Lieut. C. Wigram.

Native Aides-de-Camp, Bahauddin Khan, Risaldar Major, Sardar Bahadur, and Wali Muhammad, Risaldar, Sardar Bahadur.

Surgeon, Bde.-Surgeon-Lieut.-Col. B. Franklin, C.I.E.

COUNCIL.

Extraordinary Member, General Sir George S. White, G.C.I.E., K.C.B., V.C. (Commander-in-Chief). Took his seat 12 April, 1893.

Ordinary Members.

Sir James Westland, K.C.S.I. Took his seat 27 November, 1893.

Sir J. Woodburn, K.C.S.I. Took his seat 23 December, 1895.

M. D. Chalmers. Took his seat, 8 April, 1896.

Major-Gen. Sir E. H. H. Collen, K.C.I.E. Took his seat, 27 April, 1896.

A. C. Trevor, C.S.I. Took his seat, 2 May, 1896.

Additional Members, for the purpose of making Laws and Regulations, nominated under 24 & 25 Vic. c. 67, sec. 10, and 55 Vic. c. 14:—

Name. Date of Nomination. Province or Body represented.
Official Members.
C. C. Stevens, C.S.I. ... .. 12 January, 1895 Bengal.
H. E. M. James ... .. 20 February, 1895 Bombay.
Sir A. S. Lethbridge, K.C.S.I., M.D. ... .. 21 February, 1895 —
Alan Cadell, C.S.I. ... .. 11 October, 1895 N.W.P. and Oudh.
J. D. Rees, C.I.E. ... .. 11 October, 1895 Madras.
Non-Official Members.
M. R. Ry. P. Ananda Charlu, Rai Bahádur ... .. 17 May, 1895 Madras.
Sir G. H. P. Evans, K.C.I.E. ... .. 1 October, 1895 Calcutta Bar.
G. P. Glendinning ... .. 11 October, 1895 Burma.
Nawab Amir-ud-din Ahmad Khan, C.I.E., Bahádur, Fakhár-ud-douláh ... .. 24 October, 1895 Punjab.
Sir Lakshmishwar Singh, K.C.I.E., Mahárājá, Bahádur of Durhanga ... .. 25 October, 1895 Bengal.
Rao Sahib Balwant Rao Bhuskute ... .. 18 December, 1895 Central Provinces.
P. Playfair, C.I.E. ... .. 22 December, 1893 Calcutta Chamber of Commerce.
Rahimtula Muhammad Sayani ... .. 10 April, 1896 Bombay.
Pandit Bishambar Nath ... .. 15 May, 1896 N.W.P. and Oudh.
Babu Joy Gobind Law ... .. 22 December, 1896 —
INDIAN ESTABLISHMENT.SECRETARIAT.REVENUE AND AGRICULTURAL DEPARTMENT.

Secretary, D. C. J. Ibbetson, C.S.I.
Under Secretary, F. G. Sly.

FINANCE AND COMMERCE DEPARTMENT.

Secretary, J. F. Finlay, C.S.I.
Deputy Secretary, R. M. Dane, C.I.E.
Under Secretary, R. A. Gamble.
Director-General of Statistics and ex-officio
Assistant Secretary, J. E. O'Connor, C.I.E.

FOREIGN DEPARTMENT.

Secretary, W. J. Cuningham, C.S.I.
Deputy Secretary, Captain H. Daly, C.I.E., S.C.
Under Secretary, A. Williams.
Assistant Secretary, E. H. S. Clarke.
Attaché, Asst.-Surgeon Abdur Rahim, Hakim,
Khan Bahadur.

HOME DEPARTMENT.

Secretary, J. P. Hewett, C.I.E.
Deputy Secretary, L. M. Thornton.
Under Secretary, C. H. A. Hill.
Assistant Secretary, ex officio, G. W. Forrest.

LEGISLATIVE DEPARTMENT.

Secretary, J. M. Macpherson.
Deputy Secretary, H. W. C. Carnduff.

MILITARY DEPARTMENT.

Secretary, Major-General P. J. Maitland, C.B.,
S.C.
Deputy Secretaries, Colonel M. J. King-Harman,
S.C., and Lt.-Col. E. de Brath, S.C.
Assistant Secretaries, Captain J. Dallas, R.E.;
Major H. F. S. Ramsden, S.C.; Major P. A.
Buckland, S.C.; and Captain C. H. Selwyn,
S.C.
Accountant-General, Colonel J. A. Miley, S.C.
Deputy Accountant-General, Major W. C. F
Field, S.C.
Assistant Accountant-General, E. O'B. Smith.

PUBLIC WORKS DEPARTMENT.

Secretary, Lieut.-Colonel W. S. S. Bisset, C.I.E.,
R.E.
Deputy Secretaries, Colonel J. W. Ottley, C.I.E.,
R.E. (Civil Works, Irrigation and Telegraph
Branch); Col. T. Gracey, R.E. (Railway
Branch); R. G. Macdonald (Accounts
Branch).
Under Secretaries, N. F. Mackenzie (Civil
Works, &c. Branch); G. A. Anderson
(Railway Branch); A. R. Becher (Accounts
Branch); G. H. D. Walker (General Branch).

GRADATION LIST OF COVENANTED CIVIL SERVANTS ON THE BENGAL ESTABLISHMENT.

Substantive appointments only are stated. The names of all Civil Servants will also be found in the Lists relating to the Provinces and Departments in which they are serving.

LAW DEPARTMENT.HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE.

ESTABLISHED 1862.

Chief Justice—Sir Francis William Maclean, Kt., Q.C.Puisne Judges—Sir Henry T. Prinsep, James O'Kinealy, W. Macpherson, E. J. Trevelyan, Chunder Madhub Ghose, H. Beverley, Dr. Gooroo Das Banerjee, Amir Ali, C.I.E., C. H. Hill, R. F. Rampini, S. G. Sale, and L. H. Jenkins.LAW OFFICERS.Advocate General—Sir Gregory C. Paul, K.C.I.E.Depty. Do.—G. C. Kilby.Standing Counsel—P. O'Kinealy.Chief Law Reporter—J. V. Woodman.Solicitor—W. K. Eddis.Senior Govt. Pleader—Hem Chunder Banerjee.Supt. and Remembrancer of Legal Affairs—C. A. Wilkins.OFFICERS OF THE COURT.Taxing Officer, Registrar (Original Jurisdiction), Keeper of Records, and Accountant Gen.—R. Belchambers.Deputy Registrar, Appellate Jurisdiction—W. H. Joyce.First Asst. or Dy. Registrar—W. R. Fink.Asst. Registrar, Appellate Jurisdiction—J. Lewis.Second Asst. Registrar—Greeschunder Bonnerjee.Clerk of the Crown—J. G. Aparc.Third Asst. Registrar—J. H. Hechle.Coroner of Calcutta—Dr. E. W. Chambers.Fourth Asst. Registrar—Kailas Chandra Bhattacharya.Receiver—J. C. Macgregor.Registrar and Taxing Officer, Appellate Jurisdiction—T. W. Richardson.Official Trustee and Assignee—A. B. Miller.Private Secretary and Clerk to the Chief Justice—T. J. Waite.PUBLIC WORKS DEPARTMENT.SECRETARIAT.Secretary—Lieut.-Col. W. S. S. Bisset, C.I.E., R.E.

(CIVIL WORKS BRANCH (INCLUDING IRRIGATION AND TELEGRAPH)).

Inspector General of Irrigation and Deputy Secretary—T. Higham, C.I.E.Under Secretary—N. F. Mackenzie.RAILWAY BRANCH.Director General of Railways and Deputy Secretary—Col. T. Gracey, R.E.Under Secretary—G. A. Anderson.Assistant Secretaries—Capt. H. Bonham-Carter, R.E., and H. A. F. Currie.Consulting Engineer to Government of India for State Railways—Vacant.Deputy Consulting Engineer, &c.—F. Wolley-Dod.GENERAL BRANCH.Under Secretary—G. H. D. SURVEY AND TELEGRAPH DEPARTMENTS.

The names of the officers in the Telegraph and Survey Departments serving in the Punjab will be found serving under "India." (See pp. 30 and 31.)

POLITICAL DEPARTMENT.AGENTS TO THE LIEUTENANT-GOVERNOR.

The Commissioner and Superintendent of the Delhi Division for the time being, for Pataudia Dujana, Loharu, Maler Kotla, Kalsia, and the Hill States under Superintendent, Hill States. Do. of the Jullundur Division for the time being, for Kapurthala, Mandi, Suket, and Faridkot. Do. of the Lahore Division for the time being, for Chamba. Do. of the Peshāwar Division for the time being, for Frontier Tribes bordering on the districts of his Division. Do. of the Derājāt Division for the time being, for Frontier Tribes bordering on the districts of his Division.

HILL STATES.

Comprising Sirmur (Nahan), Kahlu (Bilaspur), Bashahr, Hindur (Nālagarh), Keonthal, Bagha Bagh Jubbol, Kumharsain, Bhajji, Mailog, Balsan, Dhami, Kuthar, Kunhiar Mangal, Bij, Darkoti, Trahoch and Sāngri. The Deputy Commissioner of Simla is ex officio Superintendent.

FRONTIER TRIBES.

The Deputy Commissioners of Hazára, Pesháwúr, Kohát, Bannu, Dera Ismail Khan and Dera Ghazi Khan are Political Officers with respect to the Independent Tribes bordering on their respective Districts.

KHYBER.

Political Officer—Lt.-Col. R. Warburton, C.S.I., S.C.

Assistant Political Officer—Abdul Karim Khan.

Commandant of the Corps of Rifles—Hon. Lt.-Col. Muhammad Aslam Khan, C.I.E.

JHIND.

Medical Adviser—Surg.-Maj. G. F. Nicholson, M.D.

PATIALA.

Medical Adviser—Surg.-Maj. C. W. Owen, C.M.G., C.I.E.

KURRAM.

Officer on Special Duty—J. S. Donald, C.I.E.

Commandant, Kurram Militia—Lieut. G. O. Roos-Keppel.

Adjutant, Kurram Militia—Lieut. G. L. Carter.

BOMBAY.

Governor—THE RIGHT HON. WILLIAM, BARON SANDHURST, G.C.I.E.

Took his seat 18th February, 1895.

Military Secretary—Maj. R. Owen, 21st Hussars.

COUNCIL.

Herbert Mills Birdwood, C.S.I., LL.D., took
his seat 24th April, 1892.

John Nugent, C.S.I., took his seat 12th July,
1896.

AT THE PRESIDENCY.

Brig.-Surg.-Lieut.-Col. G. A. Maconachie, M.D., C.M. 		Principal and Professor of Ophthalmic Medicine and Surgery, and of Comparative Anatomy and Zoology, Grant Medical College; also Ophthalmic Surgeon.
Vacant ... .. First Physician, Jamsetjee Jijibhoy Hospital; Professor of Medicine and Clinical Medicine and Therapeutics, Grant Medical College.
Surg.-Maj. L. F. Childe, M.B. ... Second Physician and Registrar, Jamsetjee Jijibhoy Hospital; Professor of Pathology and Morbid Anatomy, and Curator, Pathological Museum, Grant Medical College.
Surg.-Capt. C. H. L. Meyer, M.D. Professor of Physiology and Histology, and of Hygiene, Grant Medical College.
Surg.-Maj. W. H. Quicke ... Second Surgeon, Jamsetjee Jijibhoy Hospital; Professor of Anatomy, and Curator of Museum, Grant Medical College.
Surg.-Capt. T. D. C. Barry ... Professor of Chemistry and of Medical Jurisprudence, Grant Medical College; Chemical Analyser to Government; Professor of Chemistry, Elphinstone College.
Surg.-Maj. W. K. Hatch, M.B., C.M., F.R.C.S. Senior Surgeon and Senior Officer in charge, Jamsetjee Jijibhoy Hospital, and Professor of Surgery and Clinical and Operative Surgery, Grant Medical College.
Surg.-Maj. H. P. Dimmock ... Professor of Midwifery, Grant Medical College; and Obstetric Physician, Jamsetjee Jijibhoy Hospital, and in charge of Bai Motlibai and Sir D. M. Petit Hospitals, and Presidency Surgeon, First District.
Brig.-Surg.-Lieut.-Col. F. C. Barker, M.D., F.R.C.S.I. Presidency Surgeon, Second District and Marine Surgeon, and in Medical Charge, Elphinstone College.
Surg.-Lieut.-Col. G. Waters ... Presidency Surgeon, Third District. In Medical charge of County Jail, House of Correction, and Byculla Schools.
Brig.-Surg.-Lieut.-Col. J. Arnott, M.D., C.M. Physician to St. George's Hospital, Bombay.
Surg.-Capt. J. G. Hojel, M.B., B.S. Resident Surgeon, St. George's Hospital, Professor of Materia Medica and Pharmacy, Grant Medical College.

CHRONOLOGICAL LISTS OF HEADS OF ADMINISTRATION IN INDIA AND AT HOME.

CHAIRMEN AND DEPUTY-CHAIRMEN OF THE EAST INDIA COMPANY.

(SINCE THE REGULATING ACT OF 1773.)

Year. Chairman. Deputy-Chairman.
1773 Henry Crabbe Boulton (a). Edward Wheeler.
1774 Edward Wheeler. John Harrison.
1775 John Harrison. John Roberts.
1776 John Roberts. William James.
1777 George Wombwell. William Devaynes.
1778 George Wombwell (b) (2nd time). William James (f) (2nd time).
1779 Sir William James, Bart. William Devaynes (2nd time).
1780 William Devaynes. Laurence Sullivan.
1781 Laurence Sullivan. Sir William James, Bart. (3rd time).
1782 Robert Gregory. Henry Fletcher (g).
1783 Sir Henry Fletcher, Bart. (c). Nathaniel Smith.
1784 Nathaniel Smith. William Devaynes (3rd time).
1785 William Devaynes (2nd time). Nathaniel Smith (2nd time).
1786 John Michie. John Motteux.
1787 John Motteux. Nathaniel Smith (3rd time).
1788 Nathaniel Smith (2nd time). John Michie (h).
1789 William Devaynes (3rd time). Stephen Lushington.
1790 Stephen Lushington (d). William Devaynes (4th time).
1791 John Smith Burges. Francis Baring.
1792 Francis Baring. John Smith Burges.
1793 William Devaynes (4th time). Thomas Cheap.
1794 William Devaynes (5th time). John Hunter.
1795 Sir Stephen Lushington, Bart. (2nd time). David Scott.
1796 David Scott. Hugh Inglis.
1797 Hugh Inglis. Jacob Bosanquet.
1798 Jacob Bosanquet. Sir Stephen Lushington, Bart. (2nd time).
1799 Sir Stephen Lushington, Bart. (3rd time). Hugh Inglis (2nd time).
1800 Hugh Inglis (2nd time). David Scott (2nd time).
1801 David Scott (e) (2nd time). Charles Mills.
1802 John Roberts (2nd time). Jacob Bosanquet (2nd time).
1803 Jacob Bosanquet (2nd time). John Roberts (2nd time).
1804 Hon. W. Fullarton Elphinstone. Charles Grant.
1805 Charles Grant. George Smith.
1806 Hon. W. Fullarton Elphinstone (2nd time). Edward Parry.
1807 Edward Parry. Charles Grant (2nd time).
1808 Edward Parry (2nd time). Charles Grant (3rd time).
1809 Charles Grant (2nd time). William Astell.
1810 William Astell. Jacob Bosanquet (3rd time).

(a.) On the death of Mr. Boulton, the Court, on October 12, appointed Edward Wheeler, Chairman, and John Harrison, Deputy.

(b.) Afterwards Sir George Wombwell, Bart.

(c.) Sir Henry Fletcher being disqualified, the Court, on November 26, appointed Nathaniel Smith, Chairman, and on the next day, William Devaynes, Deputy.

(d.) Afterwards Sir Stephen Lushington, Bart.

(e.) Mr. Scott resigned the Chair, and, on September 2, the Court appointed Chas. Mills, Chairman, and John Roberts, Deputy.

(f.) Afterwards Sir William James, Bart.

(g.) Afterwards Sir Henry Fletcher, Bart. Mr. Gregory being disqualified, the Court, on July 31, appointed Sir Henry Fletcher, Chairman, and, the next day, Nathaniel Smith, Deputy.

(h.) William Devaynes appointed, 2nd December, Deputy Chairman, in place of John Michie, deceased.

CHAIRMEN AND DEPUTY-CHAIRMEN OF THE EAST INDIA COMPANY—continued.

PRESIDENTS AND GOVERNORS OF FOR'T ST. GEORGE IN MADRAS.
Names. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
William Gyfford (a) ... .. 16 June, 1684
Elihu Yale (b) ... .. 25 July, 1687

(a) Madras, which had previously been an Agency under the jurisdiction of the Presidency of Bantam in Java, was created a Presidency in 1652, Bantam being in turn placed under Madras, and the President, Mr. Baker, transferred to the latter station. This arrangement, however, continued only to 1655, when the settlement again became an Agency, and so remained till 1684, when the Agent, Mr. Gyfford, was made "President and Governor of all the English East India Company's affairs upon the coast of Choromandel and in the Bay of Bengala."

(b) Mr. Yale also acted during Mr. Gyfford's absence in Bengal from 8th Aug., 1684, to 26th Jan., 1685.

PRESIDENTS AND GOVERNORS OF FORT ST. GEORGE IN MADRAS
—continued.
Name. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
Nathaniel Higginson ... .. 3 Oct., 1692
Thomas Pitt ... .. 7 July, 1698
Gulston Addison... .. 18 Sept., 1709 Died at Madras, 17 Oct., 1709.
Edmund Montague ... .. 17 Oct., 1709 Acting governor.
William Fraser ... .. 3 Nov., 1709 Acting governor.
Edward Harrison ... .. 11 July, 1711
Joseph Collet ... .. 8 Jan., 1717
Francis Hastings ... .. 18 Jan., 1720 Acting governor.
Nathaniel Elwick ... .. 15 Oct., 1721
James Macrae ... .. 15 Jan., 1725
George Morton Pitt ... .. 14 May, 1730
Richard Benyon ... .. 23 Jan., 1735
Nicholas Morse (a) ... .. 17 Jan., 1744
John Hinde (b) ... .. ...
Charles Floyer (c) ... .. 16 April, 1747
Thomas Saunders (d) ... .. 19 Sept., 1750
George Pigot ... .. 14 Jan., 1755
Robert Palk ... .. 14 Nov., 1763
Charles Bouchier ... .. 25 Jan., 1767
Josias DuPre ... .. 31 Jan., 1770
Alexander Wynch ... .. 2 Feb., 1773
Lord Pigot (e) ... .. 11 Dec., 1775
George Stratton ... .. 23 Aug., 1776 Suspended from the service.
John Whitehill ... .. 31 Aug., 1777 Acting governor.
Sir Thomas Rumbold, Bart. ... .. 8 Feb., 1778
John Whitehill (f) ... .. 6 April, 1780
Charles Smith ... .. 8 Nov., 1780 Acting governor.
Lord Macartney, K.B. ... .. 22 June, 1781

(a) Madras having been captured by the French on the 10th September, 1746, the government of the Settlement devolved on Mr. John Hinde, the Deputy Governor of Fort St. David.

(b) Mr. Hinde died at Fort St. David on the 14th April, 1747, previous to the receipt of the Court of Directors despatch of 24th Jan., 1747, creating Fort St. David the Head Settlement, and appointing Mr. Hinde president and governor.

(c) The Court's despatch ordering Mr. Floyer's dismissal from the service was received at Fort St. David on the 6th July, 1750.

(d) The seat of government was re-established at Madras on the 5th April, 1752, four years after its restoration to the English by the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle.

(e) Governor for the second time. By order of Mr. George Stratton and the majority of the Council he was placed under arrest and detained at St. Thomas's Mount on the 24th Aug., 1776. He was allowed to return to the Madras Garden House on the 28th April for change of air, and died there on the 10th May, 1777.

(f) Acting governor second time. Suspended by the Governor-General and Council under Section IX of the Regulating Act.

(a) Acted as Viceroy and Governor-General of India from the 2nd Dec., 1863, to 12th Jan., 1864.
(b) Embarked for Calcutta on the 19th Feb., 1872, for the purpose of assuming temporarily the office of Viceroy and Governor-General of India on the Earl of Mayo's death.

GOVERNORS OF BOMBAY.
Names. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
Sir Abraham Shipman (a) ... .. Appointed 'General and Governor' on 19 March, 1662, but was prevented from landing in Bombay by the Portuguese, and died on the island of Anjediva in October, 1664.
Humfrey Cooke ... .. Secretary to Sir Abraham Shipman, succeeded him in command, and came to Bombay as governor in February, 1665. He remained in power till 5 November, 1666.
Sir Gervase Lucas ... .. 5 Nov., 1666 Died 21 May, 1667.
Captain Henry Garey ... .. 22 May, 1667 Officiating.
Sir George Oxenden ... .. 23 Sept., 1668 Died in Surat, 14 July, 1669.
Gerald Aungier ... .. 14 July, 1669 Died in Surat, 30 June, 1677.
Thomas Rolt ... .. 30 June, 1677
Sir John Child, Bart. (b) ... .. 27 Oct., 1681
Bartholomew Harris ... .. 4 Feb., 1690 Died in Surat, 10 May, 1694.
Daniel Annesley ... .. 10 May, 1694 Officiating.

(a) The first four governors held Bombay for the Crown. The Island was handed over to the Company on the 23rd Sept., 1668. For the next nineteen years (1668–1687), except for occasional visits, and during three years (1672–1675) of Governor Aungier's rule, the Governors of Bombay spent almost the whole of their time in Surat, of which factory they were presidents. During this time, Bombay was administered by an officer styled Deputy-Governor. The transfer, in 1687, of the headquarters of the Company's power to Bombay to a great extent did away with the need of a Deputy-Governor. But in spite of the change the title continued for many years to be borne by the second member of the Council. It would seem to have fallen into disuse some time between 1720 and 1738.

(b) Child was Governor and General, with his headquarters in Bombay, whither he moved from Surat on the 2nd May, 1687, and where he died on the 4th Feb., 1690. In the year 1683, Bombay was the scene of a revolt against the Company's authority. The head of the rebellion was Captain Richard Keigwin, the third member of the Council. Placing the Deputy-Governor under arrest, Keigwin ruled Bombay in the King's name from the 27th Dec., 1683, to the 19th Nov., 1684, when on promise of pardon he handed over the Island to Admiral Sir Thomas Grantham.

Sir J. Rivett-Carnac, Bart. ... .. 31 May, 1839
Sir William Hay Macnaghten, Bart. (c) ... .. ...
George William Anderson ... .. 28 April, 1841 Officiating.
Sir George Arthur, Bart., K.C.H. ... .. 9 June, 1842
Lestock Robert Reid ... .. 6 Aug., 1846 Officiating.

(a) Under Gayer, Waite, and Aislabie—that is, from 1694 to 1715—Bombay Governors held the title of General. During the last three years (1701-1704) of his nominal command, Gayer was in confinement in Surat.

(b) Proceeded to Madras on duty in Aug., 1793, and thence joined the Council of the Governor-General as Commander-in-Chief in India on the 28th Oct., 1793.

(c) Was appointed Governor of Bombay by the Honourable the Court of Directors, on the 4th Aug., 1841, but, before he could take charge of his appointment, he was assassinated in Cabul on the 23rd Dec., 1841.

NOTE.—Mr. J. F. D. Inglis made over charge on the 15th February, 1877, from which date the offices of Chief Commissioner of Oudh and Lieutenant-Governor of the North-Western Provinces were amalgamated under the order of the Government of India, Home Department, No. 45, dated 17th January, 1877 (Gazette of India, 20th January, 1877, Part I).

ORDER OF THE BATH.

* Signifies retired from the service.

THE MOST EXALTED ORDER OF THE STAR OF INDIA.

INSTITUTED 1861. ENLARGED 1866, 1875, AND 1876.†

SOVEREIGN OF THE ORDER.

THE QUEEN.

GRAND MASTER OF THE ORDER.

The VICEROY and GOVERNOR-GENERAL OF INDIA for the time being
(His Excellency THE EARL OF ELGIN AND KINCARDINE, G.M.I.E.).

KNIGHTS GRAND COMMANDERS (G.C.S.I.).

(18 Indian Chiefs, &c. 12 Europeans.)

THE MOST EMINENT ORDER OF THE INDIAN EMPIRE.

INSTITUTED 1ST JANUARY, 1878, ENLARGED 1886, AND 1887.†

SOVEREIGN OF THE ORDER.

THE QUEEN.

GRAND MASTER OF THE ORDER.

The VICEROY and GOVERNOR-GENERAL of INDIA for the time being
(His Excellency the EARL OF ELGIN AND KINCARDINE, G.M.S.I.).

IMPERIAL ORDER OF THE CROWN OF INDIA.

INSTITUTED 1ST JANUARY, 1878.

SOVEREIGN OF THE ORDER.

THE QUEEN.

LADIES OF THE ORDER (C.I.).

(With the Dates of their Appointments.)

Her Royal Highness the Princess of Wales, 1 Jan., 1878.
Her Imperial Majesty Victoria, Dowager Empress of Germany (Princess Royal of Great Britain and Ireland), 1 Jan., 1878.

WARRANT OF PRECEDENCE IN INDIA.

VICTORIA, by the Grace of God, of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland, Queen, Defender of the Faith, Empress of India,—

To all to whom these presents shall come, greeting :

WHEREAS, it hath been represented unto Us, that it is advisable that the rank and precedence of persons holding appointments in the East Indies, as regulated by Our Royal Warrant, dated the 6th day of May, 1871, should be altered in manner hereinafter mentioned, We do, therefore, hereby declare that it is Our Will and Pleasure, notwithstanding anything in Our said Warrant contained, that the following Table be henceforth observed with respect to the rank and precedence of the persons hereinafter named, viz. :—

  1. 1. Governor-General and Viceroy of India.
  2. 2. Governors of Madras and Bombay.
  3. 3. President of the Council of the Governor-General.
  4. 4. Lieutenant-Governor of Bengal, the North-Western Provinces, or the Punjab, when in his own territories.
  5. 5. Commander-in-Chief in India.
  6. 6. Lieutenant-Governors of Bengal, the North-Western Provinces, and the Punjab.
  7. 7. Chief Justice of Bengal.
  8. 8. Bishop of Calcutta, Metropolitan of India.
  9. 9. Ordinary Members of the Council of the Governor-General.
  10. 10. Commanders-in-Chief in Madras and Bombay.
  11. 11. Commander-in-Chief of Her Majesty's Naval Forces in India, unless senior in relative rank to the Commander-in-Chief of Madras and Bombay, in which case he will take the precedence.
  12. 12. Chief Justices of Madras, Bombay, and the North-Western Provinces.
  13. 13. Bishops of Madras and Bombay.
  14. 14. Ordinary Members of Council in Madras and Bombay.
  15. 15. Chief Commissioners and Resident at Hyderabad, and Agents to the Governor-General in Rajputana, Central India, and Baroda.
  16. 16. Puisne Judges of the High Courts of Calcutta, Madras, Bombay, and the North-Western Provinces.
  17. 17. Military Officers above the rank of Major-General.
  18. 18. Additional Members of the Council of the Governor-General, when assembled to make Laws, &c.*
  1. 19. Secretaries to the Government of India.
  2. 20. Commissioner in Sind.
  3. 21. Judges of the Chief Court, Punjab.
  4. 22. Additional Members of the Councils of the Governors of Madras and Bombay, when assembled to make Laws, &c.
  5. 23. Chief Secretaries to the Governments of Madras and Bombay.
  6. 24. Members of the Legislative Council of the Lieutenant-Governor of Bengal.*
  7. 25. Vice-Chancellors of Indian Universities.
FIRST CLASS.
  1. 26. Civilians of 31 years' standing and Major-Generals.
  2. 27. Advocate-General, Calcutta.
  3. 28. Advocates-General, Madras and Bombay.
  4. 29. Members of the Boards of Revenue Madras, Bengal, and the North-Western Provinces, and Commissioners of Revenue and Customs, Bombay.
  5. 30. Financial Commissioner, Punjab.*
  6. 31. Judicial Commissioners and the Recorder of Rangoon.
  7. 32. Comptroller-General of Accounts in India.
  8. 33. Commissioners of Divisions within their own Divisions, and Residents, Political Agents, and Superintendents on Pay of Rs. 2,000 per mensem or more (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts) within their own charges.
  9. 34. Civil and Military Secretaries to Governments, Madras and Bombay, and Civil Secretaries to Governments, Bengal, North-Western Provinces, and Punjab.*
  10. 35. Surveyor-General of India, and Directors-General of the Post Office and of Telegraphs.*
  11. 36. Chief Engineers, 1st Class.†
  12. 37. Archdeacons of Calcutta, Madras and Bombay.
  13. 38. Brigadiers-General.*
SECOND CLASS.
  1. 39. Civilians of 23 years' standing and Colonels.*
  2. 40. Commissioners of Divisions and Commissioners of Police, Calcutta.
  3. 41. Private Secretary to the Viceroy.
  4. 42. Residents, Political Agents, and Superintendents on pay of Rs. 2,000 per mensem or more (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts).
  5. 43. Superintendent, Great Trigonometrical Survey.

* The Governor-General in Council has determined that—

  1. (1.) The Bishop of Lahore and the Bishop of Rangoon shall take rank immediately after entry No. 18.
  2. (2.) The Bishop of Lucknow shall take rank with the Bishops of Lahore and Rangoon, immediately after entry No. 18.
  3. (3.) Members of the Legislative Council of the Lieutenant-Governor of the North-Western Provinces and Oudh shall take rank with officers in entry No. 24.
  4. (4.) When more than one Financial Commissioner has been appointed under Section 7 (1) of Act XVII. of 1887 (the Punjab Land Revenue Act), each officer so appointed shall be held to be included in entry No. 30 of the table.
  5. (5.) The Financial Commissioner of Burma shall take rank with officers in entry No. 30 of the table.
  6. (6.) Consuls-General shall take rank as No. 38 (a) below Brigadiers-General, and Consuls as No. 39 (a) below Colonels.
  7. (7.) The Director-General of Railways shall take rank with the officers in entry No. 35.
  8. (8.) The Chief Secretary to the Chief Commissioner of Burma shall take rank with the officers in entry No. 34.

† The Accountant-General, Public Works Department, and the Manager of the North-Western Railway, rank with officers in entry No. 35 as Chief Engineers of the First Class.

44. Commissioner of Inland Customs.
45. Sanitary Commissioner with the Government of India.
46. Superintendent of the Geological Survey.*
47. Inspector-General of Forests in India.
48. Standing Counsel to the Government of India.

49. Military Accountant-General.
50. Directors of Public Instruction under Local Governments.
51. Accountants-General for Local Governments.
52. Inspectors-General of Police under Local Governments.

53. Director of Revenue Settlement, and Superintendent of Revenue Survey, Madras; Survey and Settlement Commissioners, Bombay; Commissioner of Settlements, Punjab.

54. Remembrancers of Legal Affairs and Government Advocates in the North-Western Provinces, the Punjab, and British Burma.

55. Consulting Engineers to the Government of India for Guaranteed Railways, Calcutta and Lahore,† and Chief Engineers, Second and Third Classes, under Local Governments.

56. District and Sessions Judges, Collectors and Magistrates of Districts, Deputy Commissioners of Districts, Deputy Superintendent of Port Blair, and the Chief Officer of each Presidency Municipality, within their respective charges.

57. Officers in the First Class Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Covenantated Civil Service.

THIRD CLASS.

58. Civilians of 18 years' standing and Lieutenant-Colonels.

59. Political Agents and Superintendents on pay of Rs. 1,000 per mensem, but less than Rs. 2,000 (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts) within their own charges.

60. Military Secretary to the Government, Punjab, and Civil Secretaries to Local Administrations.

61. Private Secretaries to Governors.
62. Directors of Public Instruction under Local Administrations.

63. Administrators-General, Calcutta, Madras, and Bombay.

64. Inspectors-General of Jails and of Registration, Sanitary Commissioners, Inspectors and Conservators of Forests under Local Governments, and Postmasters-General.

65. Accountants-General for Local Administrations.‡

66. Consulting Engineer to the Government of India for Guaranteed Railways, Lucknow,§ and Chief and Superintending Engineers when Secretaries to Local Administrations, or to Agents to the Governor-General.¶

67. Inspectors-General of Police under Local Administrations.¶

68. Senior Chaplains.

69. Superintendent of Marine, Bombay.¶¶
70. Master Attendants.¶¶
71. Sheriffs within their own charges.
72. Officers in the Second Class Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Covenantated Civil Service.

FOURTH CLASS.

73. Civilians of 12 years' standing and Majors.¶
74. Political Agents and Superintendents on pay less than Rs. 1,000 per mensem, within their own charges.

75. Government Solicitors.
76. Inspectors-General of Jails and of Registration, Sanitary Commissioners, and Conservators of Forests under Local Administrations.

77. Officers in the Third Class Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Covenantated Civil Service.

Officers in the above table will take precedence in order of the numbers of the entries. Those included in one number will take precedence inter se according to the date of entry into that number.

When an officer holds more than one position in the table he will be entitled to the highest position accorded to him.

Officers who are temporarily officiating in any number in the table will rank in that number below permanent incumbents.

All officers not mentioned in the above table, whose rank is regulated by comparison with rank in the army, to have the same rank with reference to civil servants as is enjoyed by Military Officers of equal grades.

All other persons who may not be mentioned in this table to take rank according to general usage, which is to be explained and determined by the Governor-General in Council in case any question shall arise.

Nothing in the foregoing Rules to disturb the existing practice relating to precedence at Native Courts, or on occasions of intercourse with Natives, and the Governor-General in Council to be empowered to make rules for such occasions in case any dispute shall arise.

All ladies to take place according to the rank herein assigned to their respective husbands, with the exception of wives of Peers, and of ladies having precedence in England independently of their husbands, and who are not in rank below the daughters of Barons, such ladies to take place according to their several ranks, with reference to such precedence in England, immediately after the wives of Members of Council at the Presidencies in India.

Given at Our Court at Balmoral, this eighteenth day of October, in the year of Our Lord, One thousand eight hundred and seventy-six, and in the fortieth Year of Our Reign.

By Her Majesty's Command.

(Signed) SALISBURY.

* Now termed Director of the Geological Survey.

† The appointment at Lahore and the term "Guaranteed" are obsolete. The first part of this entry should read "Consulting Engineer to the Government of India for Railways, Calcutta." ‡ Now termed Comptrollers.

§ The appointment at Lucknow is now termed "Consulting Engineer to the Government of India for Railways, Lucknow."

¶ The Governor-General in Council has determined that:—

(1.) Vice-Consuls and Élevé-Consuls shall take rank as No. 73 (a) below Majors.

(2.) Consulting Engineers for Railways, Central Division and Assam, shall take rank with officers in entry No. 66.

(3.) Managers of State Railways, other than the North-Western Railway, in entry No. 67.

¶ In Home Department Notification No. 26, dated the 8th January, 1884, clause (b), it was announced that the following alterations had been made in the designations of the officers of the Marine Department:—"Deputy Director of the Indian Marine" for "Superintendent of Marine, Bombay"; "Port Officers of Calcutta, Madras, Bombay and Rangoon" for "Master Attendants."

SUPPLEMENTARY GRADED LIST OF CIVIL OFFICES NOT RESERVED FOR MEMBERS OF THE COVENANTED CIVIL SERVICE, PREPARED UNDER THE ORDERS OF THE GOVERNOR-GENERAL IN COUNCIL.

FIRST CLASS—(No. 57 of the Warrant).

Deputy Comptroller General.

Deputy Secretaries to the Government of India.

Deputy Surveyors General and Superintendents of Revenue Survey.

Inspector of Local Offices of Account.

Venerable the Archdeacon of Lahore.

Commissioner of Salt Revenue, Madras Presidency.

Master of Mint, Calcutta and Bombay.

Assay Master of Mint, Calcutta and Bombay.

Meteorological Reporter to the Government of India.

The Venerable the Archdeacon of Lucknow.

SECOND CLASS—(No. 72 of the Warrant).

Chairman of the Port Trust, Calcutta.

Chairman of the Town Council, Bombay.

Commissioners of Police, Madras and Bombay.

Deputy Accountants-General to Local Governments.

Deputy Commissioners of Districts and of Settlements.

Deputy Director-General of the Post Office.

Deputy Director-General of Telegraphs.

Deputy Inspectors-General of Police.

Deputy Superintendents of Revenue, Topographical and Trigonometrical Surveys, 1st grade.

Deputy Superintendent of Port Blair.

Directors of Traffic and Construction, Telegraph Department.

Educational Department Officers, 1st grade.

Examiners of Accounts, Public Works Department, 1st, 2nd and 3rd classes.

First Judges of Presidency Courts of Small Causes.

Geological Survey Officers, 1st grade.

Government Astronomer, Madras.

Superintendent of Royal Botanical Gardens, Calcutta.

Superintendent of Marine Survey.

Superintendent of Inam Settlements, Mysore.

Superintendents of Revenue Survey and Assessment, Bombay and Mysore.

Superintending Engineers, Public Works Department.

Under Secretaries to the Government of India.

Principal of the Mayo College at Ajmere.

Deputy Director of the Persian Gulf Section of the Indo-European Telegraph.

District Judges in Oudh, who are not covenanted civilians.

Commanders in the Royal Indian Marine.

Principal of the Rajkumar College at Rajkot.

Officer in charge of the Records of the Government of India.

Adviser on Chinese Affairs in Burma.

Officers of the Superior Revenue Establishment of State Railways, Class I.

Chief Superintendents of the Telegraph Department.

THIRD CLASS—(No. 77 of the Warrant).

Assistant Secretaries to the Government of India.

Deputy Commissioner, Inland Customs.

Deputy Commissioner of Police, Calcutta.

Deputy Directors of Revenue Settlements, and Deputy Superintendents of Revenue Surveys, Madras.

Deputy Superintendents of Revenue, Topographical and Trigonometrical Surveys, 2nd grade.

Educational Department Officers, 2nd grade.

Examiners of Accounts, Public Works Department, 4th class, 1st and 2nd grades.

Executive Engineers, Public Works Department, 1st and 2nd grades.

Geological Survey Officers, 2nd grade.

Inspector-in-Chief, Cotton Frauds, Bombay.

Junior Judge of Presidency Courts of Small Causes.

Presidency Magistrates.

Registrars to the High Courts and to the Chief Court, Punjab.

Settlement Officer, Sind.

Superintendent of the Indian Museum.

Superintendents of Stamps and Stationery.

Superintendents, Telegraph Department, 1st and 2nd grades.

Engineer and Electrician of the Persian Gulf Section of the Indo-European Telegraph.

Deputy Commissioners of Salt Revenue, Madras Presidency.

First Grade Officers of six years' seniority in the Royal Indian Marine, and Chief Engineers of the Royal Indian Marine.

First Assistant to the Resident in the Persian Gulf.

District Superintendents of Police, drawing Rs. 800 and upwards.

Officers of the Superior Revenue Establishments of the State Railways, Class II., grades 1 and 2.

Deputy Conservators of Forests, drawing a salary of 800 rupees a month, and upwards.

Assistant Inspector-General of Forests.

Deputy Director of the Imperial Forest School Dehra Dun.

TABLE OF SALUTES IN INDIA.
Persons. No. of Guns.
The Queen and Empress when present in person 101 By the forts and batteries within the Indian territories and seas,* and on being fallen in with by or visiting any of Her Majesty's ships within those limits.
Members of the Royal Family ... .. 31
Royal Standard and Royal Salute ... .. 31
Viceroy and Governor-General in India ... .. 31
Independent Asiatic Sovereigns ... .. 21
Other Foreign Sovereigns ... .. 21
Members of their Families and their Standards... 21
Ambassadors ... .. 19
Governors of Presidencies ... .. 17
The President of the Council of India ... .. 17
Governor-General of Portuguese Settlements in India 17 By the forts and batteries within the Indian territories and seas, and by Her Majesty's ships within those limits.
Governor of Pondicherry ... .. 17
Governors of Her Majesty's Colonies ... .. 17
Lieutenant-Governors of Provinces in India ... .. 15
Field-Marshals or Admirals of the Fleet ... .. 17
Commander-in-Chief in India ... .. ... According to military rank, with two guns more than specified below in the scale for rank.
Commander-in-Chief of Her Majesty's Naval Forces ... According to naval rank, with two guns more than specified below in the scale for rank.
Provincial Commander-in-Chief ... .. ... According to military rank, with two guns more than specified below in the scale for rank. within his own presidency only.
Generals and Admirals, or their Flags .. .. 15 The same within Indian territories & seas.
Members of Council ... .. 15
Plenipotentiaries and Envoys ... .. 15 The same within Indian territories & seas,
Lieut.-Governors of Her Majesty's Colonies ... .. 15
Vice-Admirals, Lieut.-Generals, or their Flags ... .. 13
Agents to the Viceroy and Governor-General ... .. 13
Residents ... .. 13

* Indian territories are understood to include all the waters of India within three miles of the coast thereof. Indian seas to extend from the North-West entrance of the Straits of Malacca to Cape Comorin, excepting Ceylon, and, from Cape Comorin to Aden, including the Maldive and Laccadive Islands, and the Persian Gulf.

† Personal salutes are not returned.
